title: Missing Rate Limiting
description:
A login endpoint has no rate limiting or account lockout. An attacker can send thousands of login attempts per second to brute-force passwords.

Fix the endpoint to add in-memory rate limiting: allow at most 5 login attempts per IP address per 60 seconds, returning HTTP 429 when the limit is exceeded.
hint: Use a dictionary keyed by IP address to store attempt counts and timestamps. Reset the counter after 60 seconds. Return `jsonify({"error": "Too many requests"}), 429` when the limit is hit.
explanation: Brute-force attacks are trivially easy against endpoints without rate limiting. Even a weak rate limit (5 attempts/minute) turns a minutes-long attack into a months-long one. Production systems should also add exponential back-off, CAPTCHA, and account lockout.
canonical_fix:
from collections import defaultdict
import time

ATTEMPTS = defaultdict(list)
MAX_ATTEMPTS = 5
WINDOW = 60

def is_rate_limited(ip):
    now = time.time()
    ATTEMPTS[ip] = [t for t in ATTEMPTS[ip] if now - t < WINDOW]
    if len(ATTEMPTS[ip]) >= MAX_ATTEMPTS:
        return True
    ATTEMPTS[ip].append(now)
    return False

attack_flow.label: Attacker sends
attack_flow.value: 10 rapid POST /login requests with different passwords

attack_flow.label: is_rate_limited
attack_flow.value: always returns False — no tracking, no throttle

attack_flow.label: Result
attack_flow.value: Unlimited attempts allowed — password can be brute-forced

why_blocked: Tracking attempts per IP with a sliding time window means the 6th attempt within 60 seconds returns HTTP 429. The attacker's script slows from thousands of attempts/second to 5/minute — making brute-force impractical.
